Sentiment: Neutral LLM completion: Label: Classify this review: I loved this DVD! Sentiment: Positive Prompt: Classify this review: I loved this DVD! Sentiment: LLM fine-tuning Prepared instruction dataset 77

slide_paragraph: This section divider introduces PEFT, or parameter-efficient fine-tuning. PEFT methods are designed for large models where full fine-tuning is expensive, memory-intensive, and difficult to store for multiple tasks. Instead of updating every parameter, PEFT updates a small subset of parameters or adds lightweight trainable components. This makes fine-tuning more practical on limited hardware and allows different task adapters to be stored separately. The section will explain why full fine-tuning is challenging, how PEFT reduces memory and storage cost, and how methods such as LoRA and QLoRA work.
index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 78; title: Parameter-efficient Fine-tuning (PEFT); key_concepts: PEFT, parameter-efficient fine-tuning, memory efficiency, adapters, LoRA; extracted_text: Parameter- efficient Fine-tuning (PEFT) 78

slide_title: Full fine-tuning of large LLMs is challenging
slide_paragraph: This slide explains why full fine-tuning large LLMs is difficult. The diagram shows an LLM and a stack of memory components: trainable weights, optimizer states, gradients, forward activations, and temporary memory. The bracket indicates that training can require 12 to 20 times the memory of the model weights alone. This is because optimization needs more than just the model parameters; it must store intermediate computations for backpropagation and optimizer statistics such as momentum. For large LLMs, this can exceed the memory of a single GPU. The slide motivates PEFT as a way to reduce the number of trainable parameters and therefore reduce training memory.
index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 79; title: Full fine-tuning of large LLMs is challenging; key_concepts: full fine-tuning, GPU memory, optimizer states, gradients, forward activations, 12-20x memory; extracted_text: Full fine-tuning of large LLMs is challenging LLM Trainable Weights Optimizer states Gradients Forward Activations Temp memory 12-20x weights 79

slide_paragraph: This slide introduces the basic PEFT idea: most layers of the LLM are frozen, and only a small number of layers or parameters are trainable. The diagram shows the large LLM with a snowflake symbol, indicating frozen weights, and a small trainable region. Freezing means those original weights are not changed during training, which greatly reduces gradient and optimizer memory. The model can still adapt because the trainable components learn task-specific adjustments. This approach is useful when hardware is limited, when many task variants are needed, or when we want to preserve the general knowledge of the base model.
index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 80; title: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) - frozen layers; key_concepts: frozen weights, small trainable layers, memory reduction, task adaptation, base model preservation; extracted_text: Parameter efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) LLM with most layers frozen LLM Small number of trainable layers 80

slide_paragraph: This slide shows another view of PEFT where new trainable layers are added to the LLM while the original model weights stay frozen. The right side separates frozen weights from trainable weights and notes that PEFT is less prone to catastrophic forgetting. This is because the base model's learned knowledge is not overwritten as aggressively as in full fine-tuning. The new trainable modules can specialize the model for a task while leaving the original representation mostly intact. The diagram also implies storage efficiency: for each task, we can store only the small PEFT weights rather than a complete copy of the whole LLM.
index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 81; title: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) - additional trainable layers; key_concepts: additional layers, trainable weights, frozen base model, catastrophic forgetting reduction, adapter storage; extracted_text: Parameter efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) Frozen Weights LLM with additional layers for PEFT Other components Trainable weights Less prone to catastrophic forgetting New trainable layers LLM 81

slide_title: Full fine-tuning creates a full copy of the original LLM per task
slide_paragraph: This slide illustrates the storage problem of full fine-tuning. Starting from one LLM, separate fine-tuning processes for QA, summarization, and generation produce separate full models: QA LLM, Summarize LLM, and Generate LLM. Each full model may require gigabytes of storage. If an organization needs many specialized models, full fine-tuning becomes expensive to store, maintain, and deploy. The slide's key message is that full fine-tuning duplicates the entire model for every task, even though most of the knowledge is shared across tasks. PEFT solves this by reusing one base model and storing small task-specific weights.
index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 82; title: Full fine-tuning creates a full copy of the original LLM per task; key_concepts: model copies, storage cost, task-specific full models, QA fine-tune, summarization fine-tune; extracted_text: LLM Full fine-tuning creates full copy of original LLM per task QA fine tune QA LLM Summarize fine tune Summarize LLM Generate fine tune Generate LLM GBs GBs GBs GBs 82

slide_title: PEFT fine-tuning saves space and is flexible
slide_paragraph: This slide contrasts PEFT with full fine-tuning. Instead of storing separate gigabyte-sized full models for QA, summarization, and generation, PEFT stores one large base LLM plus small task-specific PEFT weights measured in megabytes. The diagram shows QA PEFT, Summarize PEFT, and Generate PEFT as lightweight components attached to the same base model. This makes it easier to switch tasks by loading different adapters. It also reduces storage and deployment complexity. The slide highlights why PEFT is especially practical for course projects and real applications where compute and memory are limited.
index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 83; title: PEFT fine-tuning saves space and is flexible; key_concepts: PEFT weights, adapter switching, storage efficiency, MB-sized adapters, shared base model; extracted_text: PEFT fine-tuning saves space and is flexible LLM QA PEFT Summarize PEFT Generate PEFT PEFT weights GBs MBs MBs MBs 83 Generate LLM

slide_paragraph: This slide lists important trade-offs when choosing a PEFT method: parameter efficiency, memory efficiency, training speed, model performance, and inference costs. Parameter efficiency asks how many trainable parameters are needed. Memory efficiency asks how much GPU RAM is saved. Training speed depends on how much of the model is updated and how much extra computation is introduced. Model performance measures whether PEFT can match full fine-tuning. Inference costs matter because some PEFT methods add extra modules or require merging weights. The slide teaches that PEFT is not one universal answer; the best method depends on hardware, task, dataset size, and performance requirements.
index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 84; title: PEFT trade-offs; key_concepts: parameter efficiency, memory efficiency, training speed, model performance, inference cost; extracted_text: PEFT Trade-offs Parameter Efficiency Memory Efficiency Training Speed Model Performance Inference Costs 84

slide_paragraph: This slide categorizes PEFT methods into two major families shown here: selective and reparameterization. Selective methods choose a subset of the original LLM parameters to fine-tune, leaving the rest frozen. Reparameterization methods represent weight updates using a smaller structure, often a low-rank representation, so fewer trainable parameters are needed. The slide cites a guide on PEFT and prepares for the introduction of LoRA, which is a reparameterization method. The important idea is that PEFT can reduce training cost in different ways: either by training fewer existing parameters or by learning compact substitutes for full weight updates.
index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 85; title: PEFT methods - selective and reparameterization; key_concepts: PEFT methods, selective tuning, reparameterization, low-rank representation, trainable subset; extracted_text: Source: Lialin et al. 2023, “Scaling Down to Scale Up: A Guide to Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning”, PEFT methods Select subset of initial LLM parameters to fine-tune Selective Reparameterize model weights using a low-rank representation Reparameterization 85

slide_title: PEFT methods - additive, selective, and reparameterization
slide_paragraph: This slide expands the PEFT taxonomy. Additive methods add trainable layers or parameters, such as adapters, soft prompts, or prompt tuning. Selective methods fine-tune only selected original parameters. Reparameterization methods, such as LoRA, express weight updates through a low-rank representation. The diagram helps students organize many PEFT names into understandable categories. Additive methods attach new components; selective methods choose parts of the old model; reparameterization methods change how the update is represented. LoRA is highlighted because it becomes the main technical focus of the next section.
index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 86; title: PEFT methods - additive, selective, and reparameterization; key_concepts: additive PEFT, adapters, soft prompts, prompt tuning, LoRA, PEFT taxonomy; extracted_text: PEFT methods Add trainable layers or parameters to model Additive Adapters Soft Prompts Prompt Tuning Selective Select subset of initial LLM parameters to fine-tune Reparameterize model weights using a low-rank representation LoRA Reparameterization Source: Lialin et al. 2023, “Scaling Down to Scale Up: A Guide to Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning”, 86

slide_title: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models (LoRA)
slide_paragraph: This section divider introduces LoRA, Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models. LoRA is a PEFT method that keeps the original model weights frozen and trains small low-rank matrices that represent the update to selected weight matrices. Instead of learning a full large weight update, LoRA learns two smaller matrices whose product approximates that update. This greatly reduces the number of trainable parameters while preserving strong performance. The following slides explain LoRA in the context of Transformer attention layers and show how the low-rank matrices are trained and merged for inference.
index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 87; title: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models (LoRA); key_concepts: LoRA, low-rank adaptation, frozen base weights, rank decomposition, parameter-efficient tuning; extracted_text: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models (LoRA) 87

slide_paragraph: This slide recaps the basic Transformer architecture before introducing LoRA. It shows an encoder and decoder with embeddings at the input side and a softmax output at the end. The example input is French text, 'J'aime l'apprentissage automatique', represented by token IDs such as 2345, 3425, and 3853. The purpose is to remind students that text is first tokenized and embedded, then processed through Transformer blocks, and finally converted into output probabilities. This recap is necessary because LoRA is usually applied inside Transformer weight matrices, especially attention projections, rather than to raw text directly.
index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 88; title: Transformers recap - encoder-decoder structure; key_concepts: Transformer recap, encoder, decoder, embedding, softmax output, token IDs; extracted_text: Transformers: recap Decoder Encoder Embedding Embedding Softmax output Inputs Output 2345 3425 3853 J'aime l'apprentissage automatique 88

slide_title: Transformers recap - attention and feed-forward layers
slide_paragraph: This slide adds more internal detail to the Transformer recap by showing self-attention and feed-forward networks inside both encoder and decoder. Self-attention lets tokens interact and compute contextual representations. The feed-forward network then transforms each token representation through learned linear layers and nonlinearities. These components contain large weight matrices, which are expensive to fully fine-tune in a big model. The slide prepares students to understand where LoRA can be inserted: rather than changing all Transformer weights, LoRA can target specific matrices within attention or feed-forward layers.
index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 89; title: Transformers recap - attention and feed-forward layers; key_concepts: self-attention, feed-forward network, Transformer layers, weight matrices, LoRA insertion point; extracted_text: Transformers: recap Encoder Decoder Embedding Embedding Softmax output Inputs Output Feed forward network Feed forward network Self-attention Self-attention 2345 3425 3853 89

slide_title: LoRA: Low Rank Adaptation of LLMs - freezing original weights
slide_paragraph: This slide focuses on a self-attention component where weights are applied to embedding vectors. The first LoRA step is to freeze most of the original LLM weights. In practice, this means the large pre-trained weight matrix stays unchanged during fine-tuning. Freezing is important because it preserves the base model and avoids storing gradients and optimizer states for that full matrix. The task-specific learning will happen through additional low-rank matrices introduced in the next slide. This slide therefore establishes the central PEFT principle: do not retrain the whole model unless necessary.

Freeze most of the original LLM weights. LoRA: Low Rank Adaption of LLMs 90

slide_paragraph: This slide adds steps two and three of LoRA. After freezing most original weights, two rank decomposition matrices are injected. These smaller matrices are trained while the original large matrix stays fixed. The rank r is a small dimension, typically values such as 4, 8, or 64. A smaller rank means fewer trainable parameters and lower memory cost; a larger rank gives the update more capacity but costs more. The plus sign indicates that the learned low-rank update will be added to the original weight transformation. The intuition is that many useful task adaptations can be represented by a low-dimensional update rather than a full matrix.

Freeze most of the original LLM weights. 2. Inject 2 rank decomposition matrices 3. Train the weights of the smaller matrices LoRA: Low Rank Adaption of LLMs + Rank r is small dimension, typically 4, 8 … 64 91

slide_title: LoRA: updating the model for inference
slide_paragraph: This slide explains how LoRA is used after training. The two low-rank matrices, often called A and B, are multiplied to form the update B x A. This product has the same shape as the original weight matrix, even though A and B individually are much smaller. The product is then added to the original frozen weights before or during inference. Conceptually, the model uses updated weights equal to the base weights plus the LoRA update. This allows the model to behave as if it had been fine-tuned for the task, while only the small LoRA matrices needed to be trained and stored.

Freeze most of the original LLM weights. 2. Inject 2 rank decomposition matrices 3. Train the weights of the smaller matrices LoRA: Low Rank Adaption of LLMs + Steps to update model for inference 1. Matrix multiply the low rank matrices B x A + 2. Add to original weights B x A = * B A 92

slide_paragraph: This slide continues the LoRA inference procedure and explicitly shows the updated weights. The low-rank product B x A is added to the original weights to create a task-adapted version of the transformation. Because the original weights remain available, different LoRA adapters can be applied for different tasks without retraining or duplicating the whole model. The key mathematical idea is additive adaptation: new behavior comes from adding a learned low-rank correction to a frozen pre-trained matrix. This makes LoRA both storage-efficient and operationally flexible.

Add to original weights B x A + Updated weights B x A = * B A 93

slide_title: Concrete example using base Transformer as reference
slide_paragraph: This slide gives a numerical example of LoRA parameter savings using the base Transformer dimensions from Vaswani et al. The original Transformer weight matrix has dimensions d x k = 512 x 64, giving 32,768 trainable parameters if fully fine-tuned. With LoRA rank r = 8, matrix A has dimensions 8 x 64, which is 512 parameters, and matrix B has dimensions 512 x 8, which is 4,096 parameters. Together, LoRA trains 4,608 parameters instead of 32,768. The slide states this is an 86% reduction in trainable parameters. The example makes the memory benefit concrete: low rank drastically reduces what must be learned.

slide_title: LoRA for different tasks - task A
slide_paragraph: This slide shows how LoRA supports different task-specific adaptations. The base self-attention weights are shared, while different low-rank decomposition matrices can be trained for different tasks. For Task A, the LoRA matrices are multiplied and added to the frozen base weights before inference. This produces updated weights specialized for Task A. The advantage is that the same LLM can serve many tasks by swapping small adapters. Instead of storing a full model for every task, we store a compact LoRA update for each task and apply it when needed.

Train different rank decomposition matrices for different tasks 2. Update weights before inference * = Task A + 95

slide_title: LoRA for different tasks - task A and task B
slide_paragraph: This slide extends the previous idea by showing separate LoRA updates for Task A and Task B. Each task has its own low-rank matrices, and each produces a different updated weight matrix when added to the same base model. This illustrates the flexibility of LoRA in multi-application settings. For example, one adapter might specialize the model for summarization, while another specializes it for question answering. The base model remains unchanged; only the selected adapter changes. This design is useful for deployment because adapters are small, modular, and easier to manage than full fine-tuned models.

Train different rank decomposition matrices for different tasks 2. Update weights before inference = Task A = Task B + + * * 96

slide_title: LoRA for different tasks - updated weights for task B
slide_paragraph: This slide highlights the updated weights for Task B. The visual shows that Task B has its own low-rank update, separate from Task A. At inference time, applying the Task B update changes the model's behavior toward the Task B objective. The important concept is task isolation: different adapters can encode different behaviors without overwriting each other or the base model. This reduces catastrophic forgetting and makes experimentation easier because a poor adapter can be removed or replaced without damaging the original LLM.

Train different rank decomposition matrices for different tasks 2. Update weights before inference = Task A = Task B + + * * 97

slide_title: Sample ROUGE metrics for full vs. LoRA fine-tuning - baseline
slide_paragraph: This slide begins a quantitative comparison for FLAN-T5 dialogue summarization. The base model ROUGE scores are shown for flan_t5_base, including ROUGE-1, ROUGE-2, ROUGE-L, and ROUGE-Lsum. These scores represent the model before instruction fine-tuning or LoRA adaptation for the dialogue summarization task. Establishing a baseline is essential: without the base score, we cannot know whether fine-tuning improved performance. The slide also links the LoRA discussion back to evaluation metrics introduced earlier, showing that PEFT methods should be judged empirically rather than assumed to work.

slide_title: Sample ROUGE metrics for full vs. LoRA fine-tuning - comparison
slide_paragraph: This slide compares three sets of ROUGE scores: the base FLAN-T5 model, a LoRA fine-tuned model, and a fully fine-tuned model. The base ROUGE-1 is around 0.2334, while the LoRA model improves to around 0.4081, and the full fine-tune reaches around 0.4216. Similar improvements appear for ROUGE-2 and ROUGE-L. The slide notes that LoRA achieves a large gain over the baseline and is only slightly below full fine-tuning, with an indicated small difference of about -3.20% relative to full fine-tuning. The key message is practical: LoRA can recover most of the performance benefit while training far fewer parameters.

slide_paragraph: This slide discusses the choice of LoRA rank r. The table compares ranks such as 1, 2, 4, 8, 16, and higher values against validation loss and metrics like BLEU, NIST, METEOR, ROUGE-L, and CIDEr. The highlighted results suggest that performance improves up to a point and then plateaus, meaning very high ranks may not provide much additional benefit. The slide also notes that the relationship between rank and dataset size needs more empirical data. In practice, rank is a hyperparameter: small ranks are cheaper and often sufficient, while larger ranks may help more complex tasks but cost more memory and training time.

slide_paragraph: This slide introduces QLoRA, Quantized LoRA, as a further memory-saving method. It uses 4-bit NormalFloat, or nf4, for quantizing the base model. It also supports double quantization, which reduces memory by about 0.4 bits per parameter, corresponding to roughly 3 GB for a 65B model. Unified GPU-CPU memory management helps reduce GPU memory pressure, and LoRA adapters can be applied at every layer, not only attention layers. The goal is to minimize accuracy trade-off while enabling fine-tuning of very large models on much smaller hardware than full fine-tuning would require.
index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 101; title: QLoRA: Quantized LoRA; key_concepts: QLoRA, 4-bit NormalFloat, nf4, double quantization, GPU-CPU memory, large-model fine-tuning; extracted_text: QLoRA: Quantized LoRA Source: Dettmers et al. 2023, “QLoRA: Efficient Finetuning of Quantized LLMs” ● Introduces 4-bit NormalFloat (nf4) data type for 4-bit quantization ● Supports double-quantization to reduce memory ~0.4 bits per parameter (~3 GB for a 65B model) ● Unified GPU-CPU memory management reduces GPU memory usage ● LoRA adapters at every layer - not just attention layers ● Minimizes accuracy trade-off 101

slide_paragraph: This slide visually compares full fine-tuning, LoRA, and QLoRA. Full fine-tuning uses a 16-bit Transformer and large 32-bit optimizer states because all parameters are updated. LoRA keeps the 16-bit base model and trains smaller adapter matrices. QLoRA goes further by quantizing the base Transformer model to 4-bit precision and using paged optimizers to handle memory spikes, while still training adapters. The arrows in the diagram distinguish parameter updates, gradient flow, and paging flow. The main lesson is that QLoRA reduces memory more aggressively than LoRA by combining adapter training with low-bit quantization of the frozen base model.
index_text: lecture: LLM Lecture 4; slide: 102; title: QLoRA: Quantized LoRA - memory diagram; key_concepts: full fine-tuning, LoRA, QLoRA, 4-bit Transformer, paged optimizer, memory comparison; extracted_text: QLoRA: Quantized LoRA Source: Dettmers et al. 2023, “QLoRA: Efficient Finetuning of Quantized LLMs” 102

slide_title: QLoRA: Quantized LoRA - method comparison table
slide_paragraph: This slide summarizes full fine-tuning, LoRA, and QLoRA in a table. Full fine-tuning uses a 16-bit base model and trains all parameters, requiring very high GPU memory but offering maximum flexibility. LoRA uses a 16-bit base model and trains only small adapter matrices, giving moderate memory usage and faster training. QLoRA uses a 4-bit quantized base model and trains only adapters, giving very low GPU memory usage. The table emphasizes QLoRA's key advantage: it can enable fine-tuning huge models on one GPU by using quantization and paged optimizers to handle memory spikes. This makes QLoRA highly practical for constrained environments.

slide_paragraph: This final content slide summarizes the PEFT taxonomy. Additive methods add trainable layers or parameters, including adapters, soft prompts, and prompt tuning. Selective methods fine-tune only a subset of the original LLM parameters. Reparameterization methods represent updates compactly, and LoRA is the key example shown. The slide ties together the lecture's PEFT section: parameter-efficient methods reduce memory and storage requirements, make task-specific adaptation more flexible, and can reduce catastrophic forgetting compared with full fine-tuning. Students should leave this slide understanding that PEFT is a family of methods, not only LoRA, and that the method choice depends on the task and constraints.
